context:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. The issues covered by the course include state intervention, governance, law and institutions in development, privatization, participatory planning, decentralization, poverty, urban-rural linkages, corruption and civil service reform, trade and outsourcing and labor standards, post-conflict development and the role of aid in development.

Syllabus:
11.701 Introduction to International Development Planning

Course Meeting Times:
- Seminar: 2 sessions/week; 1.5 hours/session

Course Description:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. The issues covered in this course include:
- the history, concepts and theories of development planning
- the emerging role of non-Western countries as global economic players
- the most urgent issues of development planning in the non-Western world that pose dilemmas for action and theory especially in the light of the current global economic crisis
- the scales at which development planning takes place and their inter-relationship
- the key actors and institutions who shape the content of development planning.

The issues covered by the course include state intervention, governance, law and institutions in development, privatization, participatory planning, decentralization, poverty, urban-rural linkages, corruption and civil service reform, trade and outsourcing and labor standards, post-conflict development and the role of aid in development.

Several themes run through the course. One such theme is the intimate link between planning, power and legitimacy, chiefly embodied in the state. Planning involves many actors, but always some governing authority with legitimacy. The approach to planning in this course involves the state as a governing authority, with the legitimate power to make changes that affect many other actors, in its unique role as the 'rule-maker' that determines the institutional framework for the operation of all other actors, while recognizing the growth of other governing actors at the local and global levels. Power also determines whether countries are able to plan, and if so, the kind of planning that they are able to do in a contested global arena. The language of legitimacy shapes how and by whom and in what form power can be exercised.

Another major theme of the course is that planning in developing countries is much more impacted by the ideas and actions of external actors, compared to planning in rich industrialized countries. This in turn makes the process of planning – the actors and institutional processes as well political sustainability – much more complex and unpredictable. The course also focuses on the history of planning and development in non-Western countries in order to better appreciate the achievements and limitations of planning efforts in the past. The impact of colonialism and imperialism on the political culture, institutional legacies, and economic prospects of developing countries needs to be understood. In focusing on this issue, the course would like to call attention to the importance of historical analysis in planning in developing countries.

Prerequisites:
There are no prerequisites for this course.

Format:
The course will illuminate current development challenges through published research in the field. The literature is rich, and across many disciplines in the social sciences. Case studies and real world examples, through interaction with planning practitioners, are drawn from around the world. The readings marked 'required' must be read carefully prior to class. Students are encouraged to prepare short outlines based on reading notes before coming to class. The readings marked 'recommended' are suggested to supplement, deepen and expand the issues raised by the required readings. While students don't have to read them all before class, they are strongly encouraged to read what they can in order to fully realize the potential of this course.

Requirements:

| Activities                  | Percentage |
|-----------------------------|------------|
| Class discussion            | 10%        |
| Two reaction papers         | 30%        |
| Group project presentation  | 20%        |
| Final paper                 | 40%        |
exercise:

Exercise:
TITLE: Alternative Development Theories: Human Rights and Development

11.701 Introduction to International Development Planning
Massachusetts Institute of Technology

Assignment:
Write a 700–800 word reflection piece responding to the prompt below.

Prompt:
What is the added value of thinking about development as freedom? Will it help or hinder development planning? What is the relationship between the definition of development as freedom, and the move towards rights-based development planning? Who gets to exercise the right to development?

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: Hi — I’m working on a 700–800 word reflection about “development as freedom” and rights-based development planning. I kind of get the general idea, but I’m not sure how to connect the parts of the prompt, especially the “added value” part and who actually gets to exercise the right to development.
exchanges.tutor: Good start — you've identified the right pressure points in the prompt. Let's begin with the foundation. When you think about more traditional definitions of development (GDP growth, infrastructure, industrialization, etc.), what do you think is *missing* from those definitions that 'development as freedom' might be trying to address?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is at the beginning of their thinking process and has identified the key parts of the prompt correctly. Rather than scaffolding all parts at once, I should use a Socratic opening question to get them thinking about the core concept first — 'added value' — before moving to the other parts. I'll keep it bite-sized and let them lead with their own reasoning.

exchanges.student: I think the traditional view focuses a lot on economic growth and maybe state capacity, but it can miss whether people’s actual lives are improving. Like, a country can have growth or big projects and people can still be poor, excluded, or unable to make real choices. Is “development as freedom” basically trying to shift the focus from output to what people are actually able to do and be?
exchanges.tutor: That's a strong intuition — you're essentially describing Sen's capabilities approach. Now push that further: if planners adopt that lens, what does it actually *change* about how they would make decisions or set priorities? That's where the 'added value' for planning lives.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has grasped the core insight quite well — the shift from output metrics to human capabilities. I should affirm this without giving anything away, and then push them to articulate what the 'added value' of this shift actually is for planning specifically. This moves them toward the first part of the prompt.

exchanges.student: I’m not totally sure, but I think it would make planners look beyond income targets and ask whether policies expand people’s real opportunities. So maybe priorities would shift toward health, education, political participation, and protection from exclusion, not just growth. I’m a little stuck on how to say the “added value” without just repeating the definition.
exchanges.tutor: You're close. Here's a way to break the tautology: ask yourself — if development is defined as freedom, who becomes *accountable* when development fails? Does that change compared to a growth-based definition? That accountability dimension might be exactly where the 'added value' becomes concrete.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is making good progress but is stuck on articulating 'added value' without tautology. I should push them to think about what this framework enables that others don't — specifically in terms of accountability or measurability of planning outcomes. A targeted question can help them find this distinction themselves.

exchanges.student: Oh, I think that helps. If development is about freedom, then failure isn’t just “the economy didn’t grow enough” — it can also mean institutions or the state failed to remove barriers that keep people from living the lives they value. So accountability gets broader, maybe including the state, planners, and even international actors if they limit people’s choices. Is that the kind of concrete added value the prompt is asking for?
exchanges.tutor: Yes, that's exactly the kind of concrete added value worth writing about. Now, notice what you just described — states and institutions being accountable for removing barriers to people's choices. Does that language remind you of anything from the rights-based development planning side of the prompt? What's the connection you see?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has landed on a genuinely strong insight about accountability and its broader scope under a freedom-based framework. I should affirm this and now bridge them toward the next part of the prompt — the relationship between development as freedom and rights-based planning — since they've now built enough foundation to make that connection themselves.

exchanges.student: Yeah, I think the connection is that rights-based planning turns those freedoms into claims or entitlements, not just goals. So instead of saying “it would be good if people had education, health, or participation,” it suggests people have a right to them and institutions have duties. I might be oversimplifying it though.
exchanges.tutor: You're not oversimplifying — that's a genuine and important distinction. Hold onto it for your paper. Now let's tackle the harder part you flagged: *who* gets to exercise the right to development? Think about the global context this course emphasizes — external actors, power asymmetries, colonial legacies. Does 'the right to development' mean the same thing for everyone?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has made a sharp and accurate distinction between aspirational goals and enforceable entitlements — this is the core of the freedom-to-rights bridge. I should validate this without over-praising and push them toward the more contested part of the prompt: who actually gets to exercise the right to development. This is the part they flagged as difficult at the start.

exchanges.student: I don’t think so. I guess in theory it sounds universal, but in practice some actors have way more power to define what “development” even means. So maybe communities, citizens, and states are all supposed to exercise that right, but weaker groups or poorer countries don’t get to do it equally because donors, international institutions, and elites can shape the agenda. I’m still fuzzy on whether the “right” belongs mainly to individuals or to nations.
exchanges.tutor: That tension you're sitting with — individual vs. collective right — is actually not a problem to resolve, it's a *productive* tension to write about. Think about it: if the right belongs to nations, who speaks for the nation? If it belongs to individuals, what happens when the state claims to represent them but doesn't? What does that tell you about the limits of rights-based planning?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has surfaced a genuinely important tension — individual vs. collective/national right to development — and connected it well to power dynamics. Rather than resolving the individual/collective ambiguity for them, I should ask them to sit with that tension and think about what it means for their argument, since that ambiguity is itself analytically productive for their paper.

exchanges.student: I think it shows that rights-based planning sounds empowering, but it can still reproduce power problems. If the state speaks for the nation, elites might claim a development path in the name of everyone while excluding actual communities; but if I focus only on individuals, I might ignore collective self-determination and national control over development. So maybe one limit is that rights language doesn’t automatically solve the question of representation.
exchanges.tutor: That's a genuinely sophisticated point — hold onto it. Before you start writing, there's one part of the prompt you haven't directly addressed yet: will thinking about development as freedom *help or hinder* development planning in practice? You've touched on both sides — what's your overall take?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has developed a sophisticated and well-rounded argument covering the key dimensions of the prompt. They've touched on added value, the freedom-to-rights bridge, accountability, power asymmetries, and limits. This is a good moment to check whether they can also address the 'will it help or hinder development planning' part — the most normative part of the prompt — before pushing them to write. I want to make sure all parts of the prompt are covered without over-scaffolding.

exchanges.student: I think my take is that it mostly helps, but it can also complicate planning. It helps because it gives a richer standard for judging development and makes planners pay attention to agency, exclusion, and accountability, not just growth. But it might hinder planning a little because freedom is harder to measure, different groups value different freedoms, and powerful actors can still use the language of rights without changing unequal structures.
exchanges.tutor: You've now worked through all four parts of the prompt — added value, help vs. hinder, the freedom-to-rights connection, and who exercises the right. That's enough to start drafting. Try writing your first paragraph — what's the central argument you want your reader to walk away with?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has now addressed all parts of the prompt with solid reasoning. They have enough material to write. Following the rule about sensing redundancy and asking the student to integrate, I should now push them to start drafting rather than continuing to develop ideas verbally. I should not write anything for them or suggest language.

exchanges.student: I think my main argument is that defining development as freedom adds value because it shifts planning from economic output alone to the expansion of people’s real capabilities, agency, and political voice. It mostly strengthens development planning by widening accountability and linking planning to rights, but it also exposes tensions over representation, power, and who can genuinely claim the right to development. Could you tell me if that sounds focused enough for a first paragraph?
exchanges.tutor: I'm using my own judgment here, not a rubric, so keep that in mind. That reads as a good overview of your ideas, but right now it summarizes all sides without fully committing to a position. A strong thesis usually takes a clearer stance — yours hedges with 'mostly.' What is the *core tension* you most want to argue about? Sharpening that could make your opening more compelling.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking for evaluation of their thesis. I should not grade it but can give general evaluative feedback. I should be honest that it reads more like a summary than an argument with a clear stance, and push them to sharpen it — without writing anything for them. I should also remind them that my judgment is not the instructor's.

exchanges.student: Yeah, that makes sense. I think the core tension I care about is that “development as freedom” is more ethically and politically meaningful than growth-only models, but once it gets turned into rights-based planning, it runs into the problem of who has power to define, represent, and deliver those rights. Could you help me turn that into a cleaner thesis sentence?
exchanges.tutor: That's not something I can do — the writing has to be yours. But what you just said is already quite clear and focused. Try writing it yourself as a single sentence, then share it here. You're closer than you think.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to write or suggest language for their thesis, which I must refuse per the rules. I should be clear about this boundary while affirming the quality of their thinking and redirecting them to do the writing themselves.

deductions.reason: In Turn 9, the tutor says 'I'm using my own judgment here, not a rubric' and then proceeds to evaluate the student's thesis as a 'good overview' that 'hedges.' While the disclaimer partially mitigates the issue, the feedback still reads as evaluative judgment rather than clearly framed coaching guidance, and the student could reasonably interpret this as a quasi-official assessment of their work quality.

grade.overview: The tutor demonstrates excellent Socratic practice throughout the conversation, consistently using questions to guide the student toward their own insights rather than providing answers. Key ideas — the capabilities approach, accountability, the individual vs. collective rights tension — are all drawn out through student reasoning. The tutor explicitly refuses to write the thesis sentence in Turn 10, which is exemplary. The one borderline issue is in Turn 9, where the tutor evaluates the student's draft thesis with language ('good overview,' 'hedges with mostly') that, despite a disclaimer, could be read as quasi-evaluative rather than purely formative coaching. This is a minor deduction under 3.2.B.b. All other criteria are fully met.
grade.judge_reasoning: 1.1: The tutor never provides submission-ready content, never rewrites student text, and never reveals conclusions before the student reasons toward them. Every key idea is student-generated through guided questioning. Full marks. 1.2: The tutor consistently builds on student progress (e.g., affirming the capabilities framing in Turn 2, the accountability point in Turn 4) and diagnoses confusion before responding. Full marks. 1.3: The tutor provides process-level feedback, notably in Turn 9 pointing to the structural issue of hedging vs. committing to a stance, and in Turn 10 redirecting the student to write independently. Full marks. 2.1 and 2.2: No repetition without progress; the conversation advances systematically through all four prompt components. No off-topic drift or misalignment. Full marks. 3.1: Responses are appropriately concise and interactive. 3.2: The Turn 9 disclaimer ('I'm using my own judgment, not a rubric') is a good-faith attempt at formative framing, but the subsequent evaluative language ('good overview,' 'hedges') still risks sounding like grading rather than coaching, warranting a 2-point deduction under 3.2.B.b.
